topic: Unit 4: Personal or Creative Writing and Reading Literary and Non-fiction Texts
subject: english-language

items.q: In creative writing, what does 'show, don't tell' mean?
items.correct: Conveying emotion or character through action and detail rather than stating it directly.
items.random_wrong: It means using only dialogue and no narration.
items.why: Students misread the advice as a ban on description rather than a call for vivid, indirect portrayal.

items.q: What is inference when reading a literary text?
items.correct: Drawing a reasonable conclusion from clues in the text rather than from explicit statements.
items.misconception: Inference means guessing randomly with no evidence.
items.random_wrong: Inference means copying out a quotation word for word.
items.why: Learners treat inference as unsupported guessing rather than evidence-based reasoning.

items.q: Does longer, more complex vocabulary always improve creative writing?
items.correct: No; well-chosen, precise words suited to the effect matter more than length or complexity.
items.misconception: The more long and unusual words you use, the higher the mark.
items.random_wrong: Creative writing should avoid adjectives entirely.
items.why: Students equate sophistication with obscure vocabulary rather than appropriate, controlled choices.

items.q: What does the L in PEEL stand for?
items.why: It's Link. “List” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which reading skill hunts specific facts?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Scanning. “Skimming” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many letters in PEEL paragraph structure?
items.why: The real figure is 4 letters. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
